Grilled European Sea Bass with Mediterranean Vegetables
Branzino with Gaeta Olives and Capsicum Sauce

Grilled European Sea Bass with Mediterranean Vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sea bass food product entry; no specific entry for branzino alla mediterranea as a composed dish with caponata and capsicum sauce. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sea bass (Dicentrarchus labrax) — grilled, with vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for branzino alla mediterranea as a composed dish. Closest defensible anchor is grilled sea bass with vegetables (A00LC). Caponata and capsicum sauce components are not individually coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composed Italian fish dish. SNOMED CT coverage of prepared restaurant dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of branzino alla mediterranea weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a grilled seabass fillet, a generous portion of caponata vegetables, Gaeta olives, and capsicum sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175159) combined with component estimates for caponata-style vegetables (eggplant, bell pepper, tomato, zucchini, celery, olives, olive oil, capers)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for branzino alla mediterranea as a composed dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for grilled sea bass (FDC ID 175159) combined with weighted component estimates for caponata (eggplant, bell pepper, tomato, zucchini, celery, Gaeta olives, olive oil, capers) at approximate dish proportions (~55% fish, ~35% caponata vegetables, ~10% capsicum sauce). Input per_100g values from the GrabFood source (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA component data and found broadly consistent; USDA-derived estimates are used as the authoritative basis. Sodium is elevated relative to plain grilled sea bass due to olives, capers, and seasoning. Values are documented as estimated. USDA FoodData Central is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Vegetable and Fish Dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for Mediterranean vegetables (eggplant GI ~15, bell pepper GI ~15, tomato GI ~15, zucchini GI ~15) and low-carbohydrate fish component
gi_source.source_match_notes: Branzino alla Mediterranea is not directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on the weighted carbohydrate contribution of the dish components: the caponata vegetables (eggplant, bell pepper, tomato, zucchini) all have GI values in the 15–35 range per the University of Sydney GI Database; the fish component contributes negligible carbohydrate. A composite GI of 35 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate for the low-GI vegetable base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 35 × available carbohydrate 4.3 g per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 5.3 g GL, which is clinically low (GL < 10).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 243 mg/100 g; elevated by Gaeta olives and capers; estimated potassium ~340 mg/100 g from Mediterranean vegetables partially offsets sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should be aware that a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium, approaching 37% of the 2300 mg daily limit; requesting reduced olives and capers can lower sodium by an estimated 15–20%, and the dish's high potassium content and alignment with the DASH and Mediterranean dietary patterns otherwise support blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (4.3 g/100 g), high protein (12.9 g/100 g), moderate dietary fibre (1.4 g/100 g), and low-GI Mediterranean vegetable base support glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the low carbohydrate load (GL 5.3 per serving), high-quality fish protein, olive oil monounsaturated fats, and low-GI vegetable caponata collectively support postprandial glycaemic control, and no significant modifications are required.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High unsaturated fat from olive oil and sea bass omega-3 fatty acids; low saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g); Mediterranean dietary pattern alignment
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Branzino alla Mediterranea is a cardioprotective dish; the combination of omega-3-rich sea bass, oleocanthal-containing olive oil, and antioxidant-rich Mediterranean vegetables (tomatoes, bell peppers, eggplant) supports endothelial function and reduces cardiovascular inflammation, with the caveat that sodium from olives and capers should be monitored in patients with concurrent heart failure or severe hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains multiple nightshade vegetables (eggplant, bell peppers, tomatoes) which may trigger solanine-mediated joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals, despite strong anti-inflammatory omega-3 and oleocanthal profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade solanine should request a nightshade-free preparation substituting zucchini, celery, and olives only; if nightshades are tolerated, the omega-3 from sea bass and oleocanthal from olive oil make this dish otherwise beneficial for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Branzino alla Mediterranea (Gluten-Free) is an Italian Mediterranean-inspired restaurant dish featuring a whole or filleted European sea bass (branzino, Dicentrarchus labrax) that is grilled over high heat to achieve a crisp skin and moist, flaky white flesh. The fish is served atop or alongside a caponata — a Sicilian-style sweet-and-sour sauté of Mediterranean vegetables including eggplant, bell peppers (capsicum), tomatoes, zucchini, celery, and Gaeta olives, finished with capers and olive oil. A blended capsicum (roasted red pepper) sauce accompanies the dish, adding sweetness and depth. The dish is certified gluten-free and contains no dairy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 137 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 8.0 g fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated fat from olive oil and omega-3 polyunsaturated fat from sea bass), 4.3 g carbohydrate, 1.4 g dietary fibre, and 243 mg sodium. The sodium is elevated relative to plain grilled fish due to the Gaeta olives and capers. The dish aligns strongly with the Mediterranean dietary pattern and is clinically suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management (low carbohydrate, low GI, GL 5.3 per serving), cardiovascular disease prevention (omega-3, oleocanthal, low saturated fat), and hypertension management with sodium moderation. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should exercise caution due to the eggplant, bell pepper, and tomato content. The dish is commonly served as a main course in Italian and Mediterranean restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in grilled seabass with caponata?
Is Mediterranean sea bass good for diabetes?
Can I eat branzino if I have rheumatoid arthritis?
What is the protein content of branzino alla mediterranea?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for branzino alla mediterranea as a composed dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for grilled sea bass (FDC ID 175159) combined with weighted component estimates for caponata-style vegetables and olive oil. The input GrabFood values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA component data and found broadly consistent, supporting medium confidence. A high confidence rating is not warranted because no exact dish match exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central (grilled sea bass FDC ID 175159 plus caponata vegetable components) at approximate dish proportions (~55% fish, ~35% caponata, ~10% capsicum sauce). Input ai_generated GrabFood values cross-checked and found broadly consistent; USDA-derived estimates used as authoritative basis. USDA FoodData Central is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composed Italian restaurant dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; component-level data exists but dish-level aggregation for these micronutrients was not performed to avoid compounding estimation error. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.6 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central sea bass and olive oil component data; these are estimates and should be verified if used for clinical fatty acid profiling. 4. Glycemic index estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for Mediterranean vegetables (eggplant, bell pepper, tomato, zucchini GI range 15–35); glycemic load per 350 g serving = 5.3 g (clinically low). 5. The input ethnic_tags ('Pasta', 'Pizza', 'Chicken Dish') were incorrect for this dish and have been removed; correct cuisine classification is Italian Mediterranean. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301116 (sea bass food product) and FoodEx2 A00LC (sea bass grilled with vegetables) used as approximate anchors; no exact composed-dish code exists in either system. 7. This dish is served in Singapore as a restaurant menu item; it is of Italian origin and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese/Malay/Tamil/Hindi). No mandatory local-language names apply under Veritas multilingual rules for Italian cuisine. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for olive-oil-based Mediterranean preparation with no industrial trans-fat sources; this is a naturally occurring near-zero value consistent with USDA data for the component ingredients.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Branzino alla Mediterranea (Gluten-Free)' is precise and unambiguous. Italian cuisine does not require Singaporean multilingual naming; romanized_local alias present. G6 satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (137.0), protein_g (12.9), fat_g (8.0), carbohydrate_g (4.3) all present and numeric. nutrient_source cites USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175159) — an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2. Source match type documented as estimated with full adaptation notes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.4), sodium_mg (243.0), saturated_fat_g (1.4), sugar_g (2.0) all present as numeric values and plausible for this dish type.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to 35 (numeric, low category). gi_category 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving 5.3. gi_source cites University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate basis with full documentation. All four GI fields present and valid.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03301116 (sea bass food product) and FoodEx2 A00LC (sea bass grilled with vegetables) present as defensible closest-parent anchors. Gap notes document absence of exact composed-dish codes. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers: what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance (diabetes, CVD, hypertension, RA), and key nutrients. Common queries, meal context, and ingredient synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium/potassium factor, specific DASH guidance), Type 2 Diabetes (low, carbohydrate/GI/GL factor, specific glycaemic guidance), Cardiovascular Disease (low, omega-3/oleocanthal factor, specific endothelial guidance). All have concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentences. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: All aliases_by_language string values verified present in flat aliases array: english entries (7 values) and romanized entries ('Branzino alla Mediterranea', 'Branzino Mediterraneo') all appear verbatim in flat aliases. Flat aliases array contains 9 entries covering alternate English names, romanized Italian forms, and descriptive variants. Consistency check passed.
